messages.content: give a title to "Data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA) showed that both Tesla and its Chinese rival, BYD, achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles in Q2. Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles (EVs) in June, an 18.72% increase from the previous year. Overall, Tesla sold 247,217 China-made cars in Q2, its highest quarterly figure from its Shanghai factory. BYD also experienced a significant sales surge, with June sales increasing by 88.16% YoY to 251,685 vehicles. This strong performance highlights their competitive edge despite the overall slowdown in auto sales in China. In June, Beijing announced 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) worth of purchase tax breaks on new-energy vehicles until 2027 to further stimulate sales and production of EVs in the country. "
"Record-breaking Sales for Tesla and BYD in Q2, Demonstrating Strong Market Performance Amidst China's Auto Sales Slowdown"
modify 'Tesla, BYD's China deliveries hit record high in Q2'
"Tesla and BYD Achieve Record-Breaking China Deliveries in Q2, Defying Auto Sales Slowdown"

shorten "The Biden administration is planning to limit Chinese companies’ access to U.S. cloud-computing services by requiring U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft to obtain permission from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers that use advanced AI chips. The new rule is expected to be implemented in the coming weeks as an expansion of its existing semiconductor export control policy. In response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips, China has also announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry. Exports of the gallium and germanium, two metal used in computer chips and solar cells, will require official permission starting from August 1, although details have not been specified. China is the largest global source of gallium and germanium, with the U.S. relying on China for about half of its supply."
"Biden Administration Plans to Restrict Chinese Companies' Access to US Cloud Computing; China Implements Controls on Semiconductor Metal Exports"

shorten "Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki said on Tuesday Tokyo and Washington were in close contact with each other on currency moves, but did not disclose any details about the discussions. Suzuki reiterated his previous warning against “sharp and one-sided” fluctuations in the yen but stated that he had nothing further to add on the matter.
UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged that inflation in Britain is more persistent than expected, but emphasized that the measures being implemented to address inflation are not necessarily wrong. He underscored the importance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms as the necessary tools to tackle inflation effectively."
"Japanese Finance Minister: Tokyo and Washington Discussing Currency Moves; UK Prime Minister Addresses Inflation Concerns"

summarize the following:

Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki said on Tuesday Tokyo and Washington were in close contact with each other on currency moves, but did not disclose any details about the discussions. Suzuki reiterated his previous warning against “sharp and one-sided” fluctuations in the yen but stated that he had nothing further to add on the matter.
UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak acknowledged that inflation in Britain is more persistent than expected, but emphasized that the measures being implemented to address inflation are not necessarily wrong. He underscored the importance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms as the necessary tools to tackle inflation effectively.

Japanese Finance Minister Shunichi Suzuki confirmed close communication between Tokyo and Washington on currency movements, without providing specific details. Suzuki restated his concerns about volatile yen fluctuations but had no additional comments. UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak recognized that inflation in Britain is more persistent than anticipated but defended the measures being taken to combat it. Sunak stressed the significance of monetary policy, responsible fiscal policy, and supply-side reforms in effectively addressing inflation.

summarize the following:

The Biden administration is planning to limit Chinese companies’ access to U.S. cloud-computing services by requiring U.S. cloud-service providers like Amazon and Microsoft to obtain permission from the U.S. government before offering these services to Chinese customers that use advanced AI chips. The new rule is expected to be implemented in the coming weeks as an expansion of its existing semiconductor export control policy. In response to the ongoing dispute with the U.S. over access to high-tech microchips, China has also announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry. Exports of the gallium and germanium, two metal used in computer chips and solar cells, will require official permission starting from August 1, although details have not been specified. China is the largest global source of gallium and germanium, with the U.S. relying on China for about half of its supply.

The Biden administration plans to limit Chinese companies' access to U.S. cloud-computing services, requiring permission from the U.S. government for providers like Amazon and Microsoft to serve Chinese customers with advanced AI chips. This move expands the existing semiconductor export control policy and is set to be implemented soon. In response, China has announced controls on the export of metals used in the semiconductor industry, such as gallium and germanium. Starting from August 1, official permission will be required for their export, but specific details have yet to be provided. China is the largest global source of these metals, with the U.S. depending on China for approximately half of its supply.

summarize the following:

China is signaling it may grant global investors wider access to a crucial funding tool, addressing a key hurdle to foreign demand for the world’s second-largest bond market.

Authorities are actively studying measures to improve risk hedging and liquidity management tools for foreign bond investors, including enabling trades in repurchase agreements, or repos, Gao Fei, deputy director-general of the People’s Bank of China’s financial market department, said at an event in Hong Kong Tuesday, without offering details. 

Global investors’ inability to use repos to leverage exposure has been a major constraint for a popular program for trading Chinese domestic bonds, known as the Bond Connect, according to China International Capital Corp. and State Street Corp.

China’s debt market suffered record outflows last year due to a widening US-China interest rate gap, though it’s starting to see a tentative return from foreign funds as fresh monetary easing revived animal spirit among traders. The call for broader access to repos, short-term loans using bonds as collateral, comes as Beijing in May gave investors more tools to hedge by allowing them to trade interest-rate swaps onshore.

“It’s very important for people to have that choice — to investors who rely on borrowing facilities — borrowing costs is very important to them,” Robert Yu, deputy head of fixed income at CICC, said at a recent industry conference in Hong Kong. “A repo mechanism could definitely add more activity to the bond market.” 
Global investors participating in Bond Connect, launched in 2017, currently are unable to access onshore repos. In contrast, repo trades are allowed under the other two older programs for foreign investors to trade onshore debt, namely the Qualified Foreign Institutional Investors and China Interbank Bond Market schemes.

Foreign holdings under Bond Connect reached 3.19 trillion yuan ($441 billion) in May, down from January 2022’s peak of 4.07 trillion yuan, according to the program’s official website.

Echoing Gao’s comments, Kenneth Hui, external executive director of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, also said Tuesday that facilitating repo trading or allowing bonds as pledged assets for derivatives will be helpful for liquidity management for overseas investors.

Many northbound Bond Connect participants are long-term investors but their cost of trading is high amid a lack of liquidity, Hui said, adding that the HKMA is working with relevant market infrastructure institutions on the technical research of such trades. 
To boost foreign demand for its bonds, Beijing introduced the Swap Connect, a trading link that grants offshore investors access to onshore interest rate swaps. Access to the swaps will let global funds hedge their debt exposure.

However, the average daily turnover under the program only reached about 3.7 billion yuan since its inception, according to data from the China Foreign Exchange Trade System on Monday, a fraction of the 20 billion yuan daily limit for net trading.

“Without the repo, one of the significant interest rate swaps scenario actually fails to play,” Shen Li, head of global markets China at State Street, said in an interview. “Most investors go offshore to amplify their leverage by using the repo market to assist further funding, and then they come back to the IRS market to lock in the funding rate, so now all that’s missing is the repo leg.”

The brand new scheme allows offshore investors to use yuan swaps priced against benchmarks including the seven-day repo rate to hedge their interest rate risks or future monetary policy changes. 

“Like the US, the repo market is important as it popularizes the collateral and also forms the benchmark rate for short-end funding,” said Stephen Chiu, a Bloomberg Intelligence analyst. “An IRS market alone would be somewhat meaningless if you don’t access the underlying funding.”

China is considering granting global investors greater access to repos, a crucial funding tool, in order to address a key obstacle to foreign demand in its bond market. The People's Bank of China is actively studying measures to improve risk hedging and liquidity management for foreign bond investors. Currently, foreign investors participating in the Bond Connect program are unable to access onshore repos, whereas repo trades are allowed under other programs. By allowing access to repos, China aims to boost foreign demand for its bonds and add more activity to the bond market. The introduction of the Swap Connect trading link has already granted offshore investors access to onshore interest rate swaps, but the lack of access to repos has limited its effectiveness. Granting access to repos would provide investors with a crucial option for financing and collateral, thereby improving liquidity and further attracting foreign investment in Chinese bonds.

summarize the following:

Data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA) showed that both Tesla and its Chinese rival, BYD, achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles in Q2. Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles (EVs) in June, an 18.72% increase from the previous year. Overall, Tesla sold 247,217 China-made cars in Q2, its highest quarterly figure from its Shanghai factory. BYD also experienced a significant sales surge, with June sales increasing by 88.16% YoY to 251,685 vehicles. This strong performance highlights their competitive edge despite the overall slowdown in auto sales in China. In June, Beijing announced 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) worth of purchase tax breaks on new-energy vehicles until 2027 to further stimulate sales and production of EVs in the country.

Both Tesla and Chinese automaker BYD achieved record deliveries of their China-made vehicles in Q2, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA). Tesla sold 93,680 China-made electric vehicles in June, marking an 18.72% increase from the previous year. In Q2, Tesla sold a total of 247,217 China-made cars, which is its highest quarterly figure from its Shanghai factory. Similarly, BYD experienced a significant sales surge, with June sales increasing by 88.16% year-on-year to 251,685 vehicles. This stellar performance highlights their competitive advantage in the Chinese market, despite the overall slowdown in auto sales. To further boost the sales and production of electric vehicles in the country, the Beijing government announced purchase tax breaks on new-energy vehicles worth 520 billion yuan ($71.67 billion) until 2027.

modify "China is considering granting global investors greater access to repos, boost foreign demand for its bonds and add more activity to the bond market. The planned scheme will allow offshore investors to use yuan swaps priced against benchmarks including the seven-day repo rate to hedge their interest rate risks or future monetary policy changes, proving investors with a crucial option for financing and collateral."

China intends to enhance foreign access to repos to stimulate greater demand for its bonds and foster increased activity in the bond market. Under the proposed scheme, offshore investors would be able to utilize yuan swaps linked to various benchmarks, including the seven-day repo rate, as a means to hedge against interest rate risks and future monetary policy changes. This strategic move would provide investors with a vital financing and collateral option, further bolstering the attractiveness of Chinese bonds.

give a title to "China intends to enhance foreign access to repos to stimulate greater demand for its bonds and stimulate activities in the bond market. Under the proposed scheme, offshore investors would be able to use yuan swaps priced against benchmarks including the seven-day repo rate to hedge their interest rate risks or future monetary policy changes. This strategic move would provide investors with a crucial option for financing and collateral."
"China Takes Steps to Expand Access to Repos and Boost Demand for Bonds in Foreign Markets"

summarize the following:

China's services activity expanded at the slowest pace in five months in June, a private-sector survey showed on Wednesday, as weakening demand weighed on post-pandemic recovery momentum.

The Caixin/S&P Global services purchasing managers' index (PMI) eased to 53.9 in June from 57.1 in May, the lowest reading since January when COVID-19 swept through the country after authorities ditched anti-virus curbs. The 50-point mark separates expansion from contraction in activity.

The data broadly tracked the government's official PMI released last week and showed a slowdown in service sector activity as demand for in-person services weakened.

After growing at a faster-than-expected pace in the first quarter, the world's second-biggest economy lost steam in April-June amid steepening deflation, high youth unemployment and sluggish foreign demand.

Business activity and new orders both expanded at notably slower rates last month than in May, the Caixin PMI showed. New export business growth also slowed but maintained a brisk pace.

Services companies signalled a solid rise in input costs at the end of the second quarter, with the rate of inflation little changed from May, while prices charged by service providers rose marginally in June.

Surprisingly, firms' optimism towards the 12-month outlook strengthened, with companies expecting stronger economic conditions and greater amounts of new work to support growth.

The rate of job creation in the services sector also edged up to a three-month high but remained mild overall. About half of employed Chinese work in the sector.

Caixin/S&P's composite PMI, which includes both manufacturing and services activity, fell to 52.5 from 55.6 in May, marking the sixth straight month of expansion.

"Employment contracted, deflationary pressure mounted, and optimism waned in the manufacturing sector," said Wang Zhe, senior economist at Caixin Insight Group. "Meanwhile, the services sector continued a post-COVID rebound, but the recovery was losing steam."

Nomura chief China economist Ting Lu said in a note on Monday there is more evidence of an economic double-dip as the second half of the year begins.

China releases its second-quarter GDP data and June activity indicators in mid-July.

China's services sector growth slowed to its weakest pace in five months in June, according to a private-sector survey. The Caixin/S&P Global services purchasing managers' index (PMI) dropped to 53.9 in June from 57.1 in May, indicating a deceleration in post-pandemic recovery momentum. The slowdown in activity was driven by weakening demand for in-person services. However, despite the subdued growth, services companies remained optimistic about future conditions and expected increased economic activity and new business. Input costs for services rose, but overall price changes were marginal. The data suggests challenges for China's second-largest economy, which experienced a loss of momentum in the second quarter due to deflation, high youth unemployment, and sluggish foreign demand. The country's composite PMI, which includes both manufacturing and services, fell to 52.5 in June. Analysts noted signs of an economic double-dip as the second half of the year begins and await China's second-quarter GDP data and June activity indicators, to be released in mid-July.

summarize the following:

A private gauge of China’s service activities retreated in June, in line with an official index that also pointed to a slowdown in the recovery of the sector.

The Caixin services purchasing managers index dropped to 53.9 in June from 57.1 in May, Caixin Media Co. and S&P Global said Wednesday.

Despite the fall, the index remained above the 50 mark that separates activity expansion from contraction for the sixth month in a row.

China's service sector experienced a slowdown in June, as indicated by the Caixin services purchasing managers index (PMI) dropping to 53.9 from 57.1 in May. This decline aligns with the official index, reflecting a deceleration in the sector's recovery. However, despite the dip, the index remained above the threshold of 50, indicating expansion for the sixth consecutive month.
